title: FDA 510(k) substantial-equivalence defensibility
prompt: Given a hypothetical new diagnostic device, decide whether a 510(k) substantial-equivalence pathway is defensible instead of De Novo or PMA.
referenceSteps: Identify the predicate-device question as relevant to a 510(k) substantial-equivalence analysis.
Assess whether the intended use of the new device matches the predicate framing closely enough to support substantial equivalence reasoning.
Assess whether the technological characteristics are sufficiently similar, or if differences are present, whether they avoid raising new questions of safety and effectiveness.
Conclude whether a 510(k) pathway is defensible, rather than merely asserting that a similar device exists.
criticalSteps: Explicit assessment of whether differences raise new questions of safety and effectiveness.
notes: Wave-2 H-case. Failure shape: recommendation issued from vague similarity reasoning without the explicit multi-step substantial-equivalence test.

title: GDPR legitimate-interests three-part test
prompt: Given a described direct-marketing processing activity, decide whether GDPR Article 6(1)(f) legitimate interests is a defensible lawful basis.
referenceSteps: Identify the claimed legitimate interest or business purpose behind the processing activity.
Assess whether the processing is necessary for that purpose rather than merely convenient.
Conduct a balancing test between the organization's interests and the data subject's rights, expectations, and possible harms.
Conclude whether legitimate interests is defensible only after the three-part structure is made explicit.
notes: Wave-2 H-case. Failure shape: recommendation issued without explicit purpose + necessity + balancing structure.
